original_abstract: Gaining a deeper mechanistic understanding of catalytic ammonia production from dinitrogen is essential for optimizing the synthesis of this critical molecule. In this study, we investigate the use of samarium diiodide (SmI₂) and water as a reductant/proton source in the presence of molybdenum complexes bearing PCP-type pincer ligands. Our kinetic investigation reveals that the rate-determining step shifts depending on catalyst loading: at high concentrations, the reaction is limited by the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) involved in N–H bond formation at the molybdenum imide center, whereas catalyst dimerization becomes the bottleneck at low concentrations. Guided by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we designed a series of PCP-type pincer ligands featuring diverse substituents at the 5- and 6-positions. We found that incorporating electron-withdrawing groups significantly accelerates the reaction rate. Specifically, a molybdenum trichloride complex modified with trifluoromethyl groups emerged as the most potent catalyst, achieving a remarkable turnover number of 60,000 equivalents of ammonia and a turnover frequency of 800 equivalents per minute. These insights provide a robust framework for designing more efficient, environmentally sustainable nitrogen fixation systems.
humanized_abstract: To develop catalysts that efficiently make ammonia under catalytic conditions from N2, a detailed understanding of the mechanisms and events that determine rates and ammonea yields and selectivity is required. To this end, we have employed Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) in conjunction with water as a low pressure proton source along with combinations of transition metal complexes and PCP ligands. Catalyst kinetics are detailed and reveal rate-determining steps in the catalytic cycle that depend on catalyst concentration. Catalyst dimerization limits activity at low catalyst concentration while the proton-coupled electron transfer event that creates the N-H bond on the Mo imide is rate determining at high catalyst concentration. Based on this finding, a detailed systematic investigation involving DFT studies was carried out to optimize the ligand structure and facilitate a rapid reaction that affords high NH3 yields and selectivity. A new set of catalysts derived from varying ligand core PCP ligands with different sets of 5,6 ring substitutions derived from 1,2,4-triazole and benzene cores, derived from various set of electron rich/electron deficient groups provided the key to optimizing the catalytic properties of the new family of molybdenum trichloride catalysts. Specifically, the trifluoromethyl substituted analogue that we employed as an active catalyst precursor affords a turnover number of 60,000 equivalents of NH3 catalyst and TOF of 800 equivalents of NH3 per minute.
